segments.title: British Defense-class cruiser counterfactual
segments.question: What if, in 1935, all British cruisers became copies of HMS Defense from World of Warships?
segments.answerShort: The panel treats the scenario as a treaty-breaking super-cruiser fleet: Britain gains dozens of large cruiser/battlecruiser-like ships, but crew demand, small-cruiser needs, treaty collapse, and capital-ship design choices become the real constraints.
segments.summary: A Bruships counterfactual asks what happens if Britain replaces its 1935 cruiser fleet with Defense-style super cruisers, exposing treaty, manpower, and force-structure consequences.
segments.body: This Q&A segment captures a clear alternate-history exchange from the live stream. The panel notes that 60-70 large Defense-style cruisers would change engagements such as River Plate, Denmark Strait, Java Sea, and Force Z planning, while also driving up crew requirements and reducing the case for old R-class battleships. The answer then pivots to what Britain would still need: smaller cruisers for dispersed stations, more carriers and destroyers, and possibly an earlier Vanguard-like capital-ship program if the naval treaty system collapses.

evidence.note: The audience prompt asks about all British cruisers becoming Defense-style ships in 1935, and the answer frames the treaty and River Plate implications.
The panel applies the scenario to Denmark Strait, Java Sea, Force Z, and the scale of a 60-70 ship British large-cruiser force.
The follow-on discussion weighs crew demands, smaller cruiser needs, treaty breakdown, R-class retirements, and possible Vanguard-like or larger King George V design paths.
